claim_chain: Odor-evoked responses in olfactory glomeruli show conserved characteristics across vertebrates
Glomerular responses are potentiated by silencing contralateral OSNs
Potentiation is mediated by release of dopamine D2 receptor inhibition
Dopamine release is affected by the number of operative contralateral OSNs
The lateral pallium participates in bilateral homeostasis of glomerular output
This represents a homeostatic mechanism to compensate for reduced contralateral OSN contribution
The mechanism may be broadly applicable to vertebrates given evolutionarily conserved features

typical_pattern: Opening statement of main finding with experimental details
Comparison to prior work in other species to establish evolutionary conservation
Mechanistic explanation of the observed phenomenon
Discussion of cellular and molecular players involved
Integration with broader anatomical or functional context
Acknowledgment of knowledge gaps or uncertainties
Concluding statement about broader applicability or significance

likely_priorities: Evolutionary conservation of mechanisms across species
Mechanistic clarity linking molecular players to functional outcomes
Distinction between injury-related artifacts and genuine biological phenomena
Anatomical and cellular characterization of neuronal types
Temporal dynamics and reversibility of observed effects
Broader applicability to vertebrate olfactory systems
likely_attacks: Incomplete characterization of juxtaglomerular neuron types in Xenopus
Uncertainty about ionic bases supporting recovery phase of negativities
Lack of evidence for GABAB receptor participation differs from rodent models
Need for comprehensive understanding of interneuron types in X. tropicalis tadpoles
Uncertainty about whether dopaminergic neurons are spontaneously active in Xenopus
Generalization to vertebrates based primarily on Xenopus and rodent comparisons
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly acknowledges several knowledge gaps: it is yet unknown whether the three classes of juxtaglomerular neurons present in rodents also exist in Xenopus; there is a lack of evidence for an equivalent neuronal type in the olfactory bulb of Xenopus; the ionic bases supporting the recovery phase of negativities are uncertain; a comprehensive understanding of the different types of interneurons present in the olfactory bulb of X. tropicalis tadpoles is currently lacking. The paper also notes differences from rodent models, such as not finding evidence for the participation of GABAB receptors. These self-identified limitations suggest reviewers would prioritize mechanistic clarity and cellular characterization while potentially challenging the completeness of the characterization and the generalizability claims.

phrase_evidence.phrase: Odor-evoked responses were recorded as changes of the LFP in an olfactory glomerulus
thus demonstrating that the processing of information in the olfactory glomeruli of Xenopus tadpoles is not exclusively unilateral
The observed potentiation was not related to inflammatory mediators associated with injury
The similarities between the recordings obtained and those described in rats indicate the presence of evolutionarily conserved morphological and functional features
it is yet unknown whether the three classes of juxtaglomerular neurons present in rodents also exist in Xenopus
Our results indicate that a major role of dopamine is mediating the presynaptic inhibition of OSN axon terminals through D2 receptors
the data obtained support a segregation of dopaminergic and GABAergic pathways
it is conceivable that axon-bearing and anaxonic dopaminergic neurons could also exist in Xenopus tadpoles
a comprehensive understanding of the different types of interneurons present in the olfactory bulb of X. tropicalis tadpoles is currently lacking
our results are consistent with the presence of a pallial connectivity controlling the firing of TH+ neurons
This finding is well aligned with previous observations obtained in mouse brain slices
Altogether, our results illustrate a homeostatic mechanism used to compensate for a reduced contribution of contralateral OSNs
It is possible that the compensation observed upon transection of an olfactory nerve takes advantage of dopaminergic signaling pathways
Considering the evolutionarily conserved features of the Xenopus olfactory bulb, the mechanism described here could be broadly applicable to vertebrates
were mediated by glutamatergic synapses
were triggered by ipsilateral stimulation of OSNs
exhibited an amplitude related to the number of input OSNs
the participation of sustained neurotransmitter release is conceivable
the ionic bases supporting the recovery phase of negativities are uncertain
there is a lack of evidence for an equivalent neuronal type in the olfactory bulb of Xenopus
we did not find evidence for the participation of GABAB receptors in inhibiting glomerular responses
in agreement with morphological observations
in agreement with morphological evidence
The gain of function was observed as early as 2 hr after injury and faded away with a time constant of 4 days
Potentiation was mediated by the presence of larger and faster calcium transients driving glutamate release from OSN axon terminals
The cause was the reduction of the tonic presynaptic inhibition exerted by dopamine D2 receptors
These findings reveal the presence of a bilateral modulation of glomerular output driven by dopamine
the compensatory mechanism described here may represent a general property of the vertebrate olfactory system
The representative glomerular odor-evoked response was obtained by averaging individual responses
The number of GFP-positive olfactory sensory neurons and the amplitude of odor-evoked negative deflections of the local field potential were related to olfactory nerve width according to linear

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, invented numbers, or author names were included. The profile accurately reflects the writing style, logic flow, and claim strength patterns observed in the discussion and abstract sections.
